_comment: Source: NASEMSO National Model EMS Clinical Guidelines v3.0 (March 2022). Respiratory: Respiratory Distress (includes Bronchospasm, Pulmonary Edema). NASEMSO national provisional base.
level_display: All Levels — Interventions Scoped Per Provider Level
protocol_reference: NASEMSO National Model EMS Clinical Guidelines v3.0 — Respiratory: Respiratory Distress (includes Bronchospasm, Pulmonary Edema)

sections.title: Patient Care Goals
sections.reference: NASEMSO National Model EMS Clinical Guidelines v3.0 — Respiratory: Respiratory Distress (includes Bronchospasm, Pulmonary Edema)
points: Assure adequate oxygenation and ventilation
Recognize impending respiratory failure
Promptly identify and intervene for patients who require escalation of therapy
Deliver appropriate therapy by differentiating likely cause of respiratory distress
Alleviate respiratory distress

sections.title: Patient Presentation
points: Patients aged 2 and older with respiratory distress due to disease processes including: a. Asthma exacerbation
b. Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) exacerbation
c. Wheezing/bronchospasm from suspected pulmonary infection (e.g., pneumonia, acute bronchitis)
d. Pulmonary edema of cardiac (i.e., heart failure) or non-cardiac etiology
EXCLUSION CRITERIA:
Respiratory distress related to acute trauma
Respiratory distress due to a presumed underlying cause that includes one of the following: a. Anaphylaxis

sections.title: EMR Interventions
sections.reference: NASEMSO v3.0 — EMR level (Emergency Medical Responder)
points: Scene safety and PPE; primary survey (ABCDE)
Oxygen via NRM targeting SpO2 94-98% if indicated
Position of comfort; hemorrhage control as needed
CPR/AED if patient becomes pulseless
Epinephrine auto-injector for anaphylaxis if applicable
Naloxone intranasal unit-dose for suspected opioid involvement

sections.title: EMT Interventions
sections.reference: NASEMSO v3.0 — EMT level (Emergency Medical Technician)
points: Blood glucose monitoring; oral glucose if hypoglycemic and alert
12-lead ECG acquisition and transmission
CPAP for respiratory distress
Aspirin for suspected ischemic chest pain
Albuterol SVN for bronchospasm component
IV access maintenance (non-medicated)

sections.title: AEMT Interventions
points: IV/IO access initiation
IV/IO fluid resuscitation as indicated
Waveform capnography
IV analgesics (fentanyl, morphine) per pain management protocol
IV antiemetics (ondansetron) per protocol
IV D50W/D10W for hypoglycemia

sections.title: Paramedic Interventions
points: 12-lead ECG interpretation
Endotracheal intubation for airway compromise
RSI per protocol
Advanced antidysrhythmic or hemodynamic support per specific protocol
All parenteral medications per protocol
Vasopressors for refractory hemodynamic compromise

sections.title: Patient Safety Considerations
points: Normal EtCO2 (35'45 mmHg) with tachypnea and respiratory distress is an indicator of impending respiratory failure

The use of nitrates should be avoided in any patient who has used a phosphodiesterase inhibitor within the past 48 hours. Examples are sildenafil, vardenafil and tadalafil, which are used for erectile dysfunction and pulmonary hypertension. Also avoid use in patients receiving intravenous epoprostenol or treporstenil which are used for pulmonary hypertension

Invasive airways do not improve bronchospasm. The airway should be managed in the least invasive way possible. Supraglottic devices and endotracheal intubation should be considered only if BVM ventilation fails

Positive pressure ventilation in the setting of bronchoconstriction, either via a supraglottic airway or intubation, increases the risk of air trapping which can lead to pneumothorax and cardiovascular collapse. These interventions should be reserved for situations of respiratory failure
The following medications should not be administered to manage bronchospasm as there is no evidence of patient benefit: i. Inhaled magnesium sulfate

sections.title: Educational Pearls
points: The combination of ipratropium with albuterol may decrease the need for hospital admission in certain patients
Magnesium sulfate may cause hypotension that will usually respond to a fluid bolus
Patient with acute heart failure and hypotension have high mortality

When assessing for cause of respiratory distress, CHF tends to be associated with lower levels of EtCO2 compared to COPD. EtCO2 values that are extremely low and high are markers of poor outcomes and need for intubation or ICU admission

Nebulizer droplets can carry viral particles and other airborne pathogens, so additional PPE should be considered, including placement of a surgical mask over the nebulizer (if feasible) to limit droplet spread

Factors that have been shown to be associated with increased mortality from asthma include: a. Severe asthma as evidenced by at least one of the following: i. Prior near-fatal asthma (e.g., ICU admission or intubation/mechanical ventilation)
Single dose dexamethasone has been found equally effective as several days dosing of other steroids, so dexamethasone is preferred over other po steroids
Acute heart failure is a common cause of pulmonary edema ' other causes include:

sections.title: Key Documentation
points: Document key aspects of the exam at baseline and after each intervention:
' Respiratory rate
' Oxygen saturation
' EtCO2/waveform shape
' Use of accessory muscles
' Breath sounds and quality
' Mental status
' Response to interventions

EMR: Scene safety, primary survey, CPR, AED, BVM, bleeding control, oral airway, O2 therapy, epinephrine auto-injector for anaphylaxis, naloxone unit-dose auto-injector
EMT: EMR scope PLUS: NPA, supraglottic airway, CPAP, pulse oximetry, 12-lead ECG acquisition, oral glucose, aspirin, patient own nitroglycerin, albuterol SVN, IM epinephrine, blood glucose monitoring, cervical collar, traction splint
AEMT: EMT scope PLUS: waveform capnography, IO access, IV access initiation, IV/IO fluids, IV medications (dextrose, naloxone, epinephrine, glucagon, analgesics, antiemetics), subcutaneous/intranasal medications, nitroglycerin from stock
Paramedic: AEMT scope PLUS: ET intubation, RSI, cricothyrotomy, needle decompression, manual defibrillation, cardioversion, transcutaneous pacing, 12-lead ECG interpretation, all antidysrhythmics, vasopressors, all medication routes and classes

deterioration_flags: Normal EtCO2 (35'45 mmHg) with tachypnea and respiratory distress is an indicator of impending respiratory failure

Positive pressure ventilation in the setting of bronchoconstriction, either via a supraglottic airway or intubation, increases the risk of air trapping which can lead to pneumothorax and cardiovascular collapse. These interventions should be reserved for situations of respiratory failure
scope_notes: EMR scope is the minimum national standard — state/MCA may expand
All interventions subject to local medical director credentialing
Pediatric patients: use weight-based dosing and age-appropriate equipment
Contact medical direction when required by local protocol or for interventions outside standard scope
